The notice of interim suspension must include: (1) a statement of the regulation(s) the student allegedly violated, (2) a specific statement of the facts constituting the alleged violation, (3) instructions to contact the Dean of Students Office to submit the Disciplinary Process Choice Form within five business days, and (4) a copy of the Student Code of Conduct or its web address.
Can a student appeal an interim suspension, and what happens during the appeal process?

Yes, a student may appeal an interim suspension by submitting a written statement of appeal to the Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students. The appeal will be considered on an expedited basis, but the interim suspension remains in effect while the appeal is pending.
What happens if a student doesn’t submit the Disciplinary Process Choice Form within five business days?

If a student fails to submit the Disciplinary Process Choice Form within the five-business-day period, they will be considered to have elected to proceed with a Disciplinary Conference under the Student Disciplinary Procedures.
Who can initially issue a notice of interim suspension, and what happens afterward?

The Chief of Police or designee may initially issue a notice of interim suspension when there is reasonable cause to believe the student poses an immediate threat. In such cases, the Hearing Officer will subsequently issue a formal notice of interim suspension in accordance with Section 11.
I just got a notice saying I’m under interim suspension. What does that even mean?

An interim suspension means you’ve been temporarily removed from all or part of University premises or activities because the Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students (or designee) believes your presence poses a significant risk of harm to people or property. This is not a final decision—it’s a temporary measure while the disciplinary process moves forward.
Do I get a chance to respond or appeal this before it goes into effect?

The interim suspension takes effect immediately, but you can appeal it by submitting a written statement to the Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students. They’ll review your appeal quickly, though the suspension stays in place during the appeal process. You should also contact the Dean of Students Office within five business days to submit your Disciplinary Process Choice Form.
I missed the five-day deadline to turn in my Disciplinary Process Choice Form after getting an interim suspension notice. What happens now?

If you don’t submit the Disciplinary Process Choice Form within five business days, you’re automatically considered to have chosen a Disciplinary Conference as your resolution method under the Student Disciplinary Procedures.
Is there any way to still request a different process, like a hearing panel, even though I missed the deadline?

You may be able to request an extension if you have good cause for missing the deadline—this would need to be approved by the Hearing Officer. It’s best to contact the Dean of Students Office right away to explain your situation and see if they’ll grant an extension.

I'm a freshman and just started this semester, but I got into an argument during a club meeting last week and campus security was called. Now I’ve received a notice saying I’m on interim suspension. What does this mean and what should I do next?

An interim suspension means the university believes your presence poses a significant risk to the safety or security of others or to property, based on the incident you described. According to Section 11 of the Student Handbook, you should have received a written notice that includes the alleged violation(s), the facts involved, and instructions to contact the Dean of Students Office within five business days to submit a Disciplinary Process Choice Form. You also have the right to appeal the interim suspension in writing to the Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students, though the suspension stays in place during the appeal. It’s important to act quickly—reach out to the Dean of Students Office as soon as possible to understand your options and next steps.
I got a disciplinary notice and I'm not sure what to do—I missed the deadline to submit the Disciplinary Process Choice Form because I was hospitalized. Will I automatically be suspended?

Missing the five-business-day deadline to submit the Disciplinary Process Choice Form typically means you’ll automatically proceed to a Disciplinary Conference (per Section 11.4). However, Section 11.3 allows the Hearing Officer to grant an extension for 'good cause'—and hospitalization likely qualifies. You should contact the Hearing Officer or Dean of Students Office immediately, explain your situation, and request a retroactive extension. Provide documentation if possible. Acting quickly may still allow you to choose your preferred process rather than defaulting to a Disciplinary Conference.
Who has the authority to issue an interim no contact order at University of Houston-Clear Lake?

The Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students or their designee has the authority to issue an interim no contact order if it is deemed necessary to protect a person’s safety and/or preserve a safe environment.
What does an interim no contact order prohibit?
An interim no contact order prohibits a student or registered student organization from contacting a specific individual or individuals.
Can the university withhold my diploma if I’m accused of violating the Student Code of Conduct?

Yes, the Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students or designee may withhold your official transcript, grade, diploma, or degree if you are alleged to have violated a University rule or the Prohibited Conduct Section of the Student Code of Conduct.
How can I appeal the withholding of my transcript or diploma?
You can appeal by submitting a written statement of appeal to the Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students, who will consider it on an expedited basis.
Does the withholding of my academic records stay in place while my appeal is being reviewed?
Yes, the withholding of your transcript, grade, diploma, or degree remains in effect during the pendency of your appeal.
I heard someone got a no contact order against them before even having a hearing. Is that allowed?

Yes, that’s permitted under Section 12 of the Student Handbook. The Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students or their designee can issue an interim no contact order immediately if they believe it’s necessary to protect someone’s safety or maintain a safe campus environment—even before a formal hearing takes place.
So does that mean I could be restricted from contacting someone without getting a chance to tell my side first?

An interim no contact order is a temporary protective measure, not a final determination of responsibility. You’ll still have the opportunity to share your perspective during the formal conduct process. The order remains in place only until a hearing occurs or the matter is otherwise resolved.
I’m supposed to graduate next month, but I was told my diploma is being withheld because of a conduct allegation. Can they do that?

Yes, according to Section 13 of the Student Handbook, the Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students or designee may withhold your diploma if you’re alleged to have violated a University rule or the Student Code of Conduct. This can happen while your case is pending a hearing or appeal.
Is there any way to get my diploma released sooner? I need it for a job starting right after graduation.

You can submit a written appeal to the Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students requesting release of your diploma. They will review your appeal on an expedited basis, though the withholding will stay in effect while your appeal is being considered.

I'm in my final year and want to make sure I meet all graduation requirements, but I just received notice that I’m under investigation for a conduct violation. Will this affect my ability to get my diploma on time?

Yes, it could. According to Section 13 of the Student Handbook, the Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students may withhold your diploma if you’re alleged to have violated the Student Code of Conduct—even while the matter is pending. This hold can remain in place through any hearing, disciplinary conference, or appeal process. However, you have the right to submit a written appeal of the withholding to the Dean of Students, who will review it on an expedited basis.
I got a disciplinary notice and I'm not sure what to do... I also just found out there’s an interim no contact order against me. What does that mean, and how was it issued?

An interim no contact order can be issued immediately by the Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students (or their designee) if they believe it’s necessary to protect someone’s safety or maintain a safe campus environment—even before a formal hearing takes place. This order prohibits you from contacting a specific individual or individuals. It’s important to follow this order strictly, as violating it could result in additional disciplinary action. You’ll still have the opportunity to respond to the allegations during the formal conduct process.
Can a student be referred to counseling during a disciplinary process?
Yes, the Hearing Officer may refer any student who is the subject of prohibited conduct allegations to the Counseling and Mental Health Center or Health Services.
What happens if a student refuses a counseling referral?
A student may refuse to attend a session with the Counseling and Mental Health Center or Health Services without penalty, and the disciplinary process will continue as if no referral were made.
Can the Counseling Center share information about a student with the Hearing Officer?
Yes, but only with the student’s written permission. The Counseling and Mental Health Center or Health Services may then discuss the results of the referral with the Hearing Officer.
What should a student do if asked to meet with the Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students?

The student must heed the request and respond at the time and place set by the Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students or their designee, unless extraordinary circumstances require alternative arrangements.
What are the consequences of not responding to a request from the Dean of Students?
Failure to report or respond as directed constitutes a separate violation of the Code and may result in disciplinary action and/or a hold on the student’s enrollment.
I was referred to the Counseling and Mental Health Center by a Hearing Officer. Do I have to go?

No, you are not required to attend. According to Section 14 of the Student Handbook, you may refuse to attend a session with the Counseling and Mental Health Center or Health Services without penalty. If you choose not to go, the disciplinary process will continue as if no referral were made.
If I do decide to go, will the counselor tell the Hearing Officer what we talked about?

Only if you give written permission. The handbook states that the Counseling and Mental Health Center or Health Services may, with your written consent, discuss the results of the referral with the Hearing Officer. Without your permission, they cannot share details of your session.
I got an email from the Dean of Students asking me to meet tomorrow, but I have a final exam at the same time. What should I do?

According to Section 15 of the Student Handbook, requests from the Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students or their designee must be heeded by all students and take priority over other duties, including exams. However, if you have extraordinary circumstances—like a scheduled final—you should contact the Dean’s office immediately to request a rescheduling.
What happens if I just miss the meeting because of my exam?

Failing to report or respond as directed by the Dean of Students or their designee constitutes a separate violation of the Student Code and could result in disciplinary action or a hold on your enrollment. It’s important to reach out proactively to explain your conflict and seek alternate arrangements.
I got a disciplinary notice and I'm not sure what to do. The letter mentioned I might be referred to counseling—do I have to go, and what happens if I don't?

According to Section 14 of the Student Handbook, if you're referred to the Counseling and Mental Health Center or Health Services as part of a disciplinary matter, you’re not required to attend—you can refuse without penalty. However, if you choose not to go, the disciplinary process will continue as though the referral never happened. It’s your choice, but keep in mind that engaging with support services may be viewed positively during the process.
I received an email from the Dean of Students asking me to meet tomorrow about a conduct issue, but I have a final exam at the same time. What should I do?

Section 15 of the Student Handbook states that requests from the Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students (or their designee) take priority over all other student obligations, including exams. However, if you have a scheduling conflict due to extraordinary circumstances—like a final exam—you should contact the Dean’s office immediately to explain the situation and request to reschedule. Failure to respond or appear without making such arrangements could result in additional disciplinary action or an enrollment hold.
Can the university require me to appear before the University Hearing Board?

Yes, the Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students or their designee has the authority to require you to appear before the University Hearing Board at a designated time and place if your presence is necessary to resolve a matter before the Board.
What can I be required to bring to a University Hearing Board appearance?

You may be required to produce any items that are relevant to the adjudication of the matter before the University Hearing Board, as determined by the Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students or their designee.
If I withdraw from UHCL while a disciplinary case is pending, am I still subject to the university's disciplinary process?
Yes, you remain subject to the jurisdiction of the Student Disciplinary Procedures even if you withdraw from the University of Houston-Clear Lake while disciplinary proceedings are pending.
Can the university prevent me from re-enrolling if I withdraw during a disciplinary investigation?
Yes, the Dean of Students Office may place a disciplinary hold on your records and future enrollment if you withdraw prior to the resolution of pending disciplinary action.
Will my transcript show anything if I withdraw while disciplinary proceedings are ongoing?
Yes, the Dean of Students Office may place a notation on your official transcript stating, 'Disciplinary proceedings are pending at the University of Houston-Clear Lake.'
I got a notice saying I have to appear before the University Hearing Board. What does that mean?

That means the Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students or their designee has determined your presence is necessary to resolve a matter currently before the Board. You’ll be required to attend at the specified time and place, and you may also be asked to bring specific items relevant to the case.
Can I just skip it if I decide to withdraw from the university instead?

No, withdrawing won’t exempt you from the process. If you withdraw while disciplinary proceedings are pending, the Dean of Students Office can place a disciplinary hold on your records and future enrollment. They may also add a notation to your transcript stating that 'Disciplinary proceedings are pending at the University of Houston-Clear Lake.' You remain subject to the Student Disciplinary Procedures even after withdrawal.
What happens if I don’t show up for my University Hearing Board appearance?

If you fail to appear as required by the Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students or their designee, the University Hearing Board may proceed with the hearing in your absence. Additionally, a disciplinary hold could be placed on your student record, which may affect your ability to register for classes, receive transcripts, or graduate.
Could they still take action against me if I’ve already withdrawn from UHCL?

Yes. Even if you’ve withdrawn, you’re still under the jurisdiction of the Student Disciplinary Procedures as long as the disciplinary matter was pending at the time of withdrawal. The Dean of Students Office may place a hold on your records and add a notation to your official transcript indicating that disciplinary proceedings are pending.
I got a disciplinary notice and I'm not sure what to do. I’m supposed to appear before the University Hearing Board, but I don’t understand why they can make me show up or bring documents.

According to Section 16 of the Student Handbook, the Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students (or their designee) has the authority to require you to appear before the University Hearing Board if your presence is needed to resolve a matter before them. They can also ask you to bring any relevant items or documents that help with the adjudication. It’s important to comply with this request—failing to appear or provide requested materials could affect your case or lead to additional consequences.
I’m thinking about withdrawing from UHCL because I’m overwhelmed, but I just received notice that I have pending disciplinary action. What happens if I leave before it’s resolved?

Even if you withdraw from the University of Houston-Clear Lake while disciplinary proceedings are pending, you’re still subject to the Student Disciplinary Procedures. The Dean of Students Office may place a disciplinary hold on your records and future enrollment, and they can add a notation to your official transcript stating, 'Disciplinary proceedings are pending at the University of Houston-Clear Lake.' This could impact your ability to transfer credits or enroll elsewhere until the matter is resolved.
Can I appeal my conduct case just because I disagree with the outcome?

No. According to Section 18.1 of the Student Handbook, an appeal is not an opportunity to re-hear the original conduct case and will not be granted solely based on a respondent’s disagreement with the outcome.
What are the valid grounds for filing an appeal in a student conduct case?

Valid grounds for appeal, as outlined in Section 18.2, are: (a) new information not available at the time of the original hearing that could affect the decision; (b) the weight of the evidence does not support or contradicts the finding of violation; (c) a procedural error that caused material harm or prejudice to the student; or (d) the sanction imposed was excessively severe relative to the violation.
Who reviews and decides on student conduct appeals?
The Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students or their designee serves as the Appellate Officer for all conduct cases and is responsible for reviewing and deciding appeals, as stated in Section 18.3.
How long do I have to file an appeal after receiving my conduct decision?
You must file a written appeal with the Appellate Officer within ten business days after receiving written notification of the decision, according to Section 18.4.
What possible outcomes can result from an appeal of a conduct decision?

As stated in Section 18.4, the Appellate Officer may: (1) amend the finding(s) and/or sanction(s); (2) remand the case for further proceedings; or (3) uphold the original decision and sanction(s). The Appellate Officer’s decision is final.
I was found responsible for academic dishonesty and got a failing grade in the course. I don’t agree with the decision—can I appeal just because I think the outcome was unfair?

An appeal isn’t meant to re-hear your case or reconsider the outcome simply because you disagree with it. According to Section 18.1 of the Student Handbook, appeals must be based on specific grounds outlined in Section 18.2.
What are those acceptable grounds? Could I appeal if I found an email that proves I didn’t cheat, but I didn’t have it during the original hearing?

Yes, that could qualify. Section 18.2(a) allows appeals based on new information that wasn’t available at the time of the original hearing and that could affect the decision. You’d need to include that email and explain why it wasn’t available earlier when you submit your written appeal to the Appellate Officer within ten business days.
I appealed my conduct sanction because I think the one-semester suspension is way too harsh for what I did. Will the appeal process reconsider whether I actually violated the policy?

No—it won’t. According to Section 18.2(d), if your appeal is based solely on the claim that the sanction is excessively severe, it only relates to the sanction itself, not the finding that you were 'in violation.' The original finding of responsibility stands unless another valid ground for appeal (like new evidence or procedural error) is also presented.
Got it. So if I only argue about the punishment being too harsh, they won’t revisit whether I actually did it?

Correct. The Appellate Officer will only review the appropriateness of the sanction in relation to the violation you were found responsible for. If you want the finding itself reconsidered, you’d need to base your appeal on other grounds, such as new evidence (18.2a), insufficient evidence (18.2b), or a significant procedural error that caused you harm (18.2c).

I'm a freshman and just started this semester, but I received a disciplinary notice for an incident that happened during orientation. I admitted to the behavior at the hearing, but now I think the suspension they gave me is way too harsh for a first-time mistake. Can I appeal just because I think the punishment doesn’t fit what I did?

Yes, you can appeal—but only on specific grounds outlined in the student handbook. Since you admitted to the behavior, you can’t appeal the finding that you violated the policy. However, under Section 18.2(d), you may appeal if you believe the sanction (like your suspension) is excessively severe in relation to the violation. Your appeal must clearly explain why the punishment is disproportionate, and you must file it in writing with the Appellate Officer within ten business days of receiving your decision letter.

I got a disciplinary notice last week saying I was found responsible for academic dishonesty, but during the hearing, they didn’t consider a key email from my professor that shows I asked for clarification on the assignment before submitting it. I only found the email after the hearing. Can I use this as a reason to appeal?

Yes, this could be valid grounds for an appeal. According to Section 18.2(a), you may appeal if you have new information that was not available at the time of the original hearing and that could affect the outcome. Since you’ve discovered an email that wasn’t presented during the hearing—and it’s relevant to whether you acted dishonestly—you should include it in a written appeal. Be sure to explain why the email wasn’t available earlier and how it might change the finding. Submit your appeal to the Appellate Officer within ten business days of receiving your decision.
For which types of sanctions does the University keep a permanent disciplinary record?
The University maintains a permanent written disciplinary record for students who receive a sanction of suspension, expulsion, denial or revocation of a degree, and/or withdrawal of diploma.
Who has access to a student's disciplinary records?

Disciplinary records are confidential and accessible only to the Dean of Students or University officials with legitimate educational interests, unless the student provides written authorization or disclosure is required by state or federal law, court order, or subpoena.
How long are disciplinary records kept if the student did not receive a major sanction like suspension or expulsion?

Disciplinary records for students who did not receive a sanction of suspension, expulsion, denial or revocation of a degree, or withdrawal of diploma are maintained for seven years after the final disposition of the case, in accordance with the University’s Records Retention Schedule and Clery Act regulations.
Can a student request to have a disciplinary warning removed from their record?
Yes, students who received a warning sanction may request that the record be removed once the warning period has expired or after they have graduated from the University.
Which office is responsible for maintaining student disciplinary records?
The Dean of Students Office is responsible for maintaining all student disciplinary records.
I received a disciplinary warning last semester. How long will that stay on my record?
Disciplinary warning records are maintained as active conduct records for the specified period of your warning. Once that period ends—or once you graduate—you can request to have the record removed.
So if I graduate next year, can I ask to have it removed right after graduation even if the warning period hasn’t technically ended?
Yes. According to Section 19.4 of the Student Handbook, you may request removal of the warning record either after the warning period expires or upon graduation—whichever comes first.
If I was suspended last year, will that stay on my record forever?

Yes. Per Section 19.1 of the Student Handbook, the University maintains a permanent written disciplinary record for any student who received a sanction of suspension, expulsion, denial or revocation of a degree, or withdrawal of diploma.
Who has access to that permanent record? Can future employers see it?

Disciplinary records are treated as confidential student records. They’re only accessible to the Dean of Students or University officials with legitimate educational interests, unless you provide written authorization, or disclosure is required by state/federal law or a court order or subpoena.

I'm a freshman and just started this semester. Last week, I received a disciplinary warning for a minor policy violation. I’m worried this will stay on my record forever—will it affect my chances for study abroad or internships?

Since you received a disciplinary warning—and not a more serious sanction like suspension or expulsion—the record will be maintained only for the duration of your warning period. According to Section 19.4 of the Student Handbook, once that period ends or after you graduate, you can request to have the warning removed from your record. As long as the warning is active, it may be accessible to university officials with legitimate educational interests, but it won’t automatically disqualify you from study abroad or internships. You’re encouraged to speak with the Dean of Students Office about your specific situation.

I’m in my final year and want to make sure I meet all graduation requirements. A few years ago, I was suspended for one semester due to a conduct violation. Will this disciplinary record prevent me from graduating or showing up on my transcript?

Because your sanction included suspension, the University maintains a permanent disciplinary record under Section 19.1. However, this record is kept separately from your academic transcript and is confidential—accessible only to the Dean of Students, authorized university officials with legitimate educational interests, or as required by law. As long as you’ve completed all academic requirements and any conditions tied to your reinstatement after suspension, this disciplinary record should not prevent you from graduating. If you have concerns, contact the Dean of Students Office to confirm your standing.
Can a student organization be held responsible for misconduct even if only some members were involved?

Yes. Student groups and registered student organizations, along with their members, may be held collectively and/or individually responsible for violations that occur on University grounds, on premises used or controlled by the group or organization, or at University-sponsored activities on or off campus.
Who handles conduct cases for student organizations versus individual students?

Conduct matters for student groups and registered student organizations are adjudicated by the University Hearing Board, whereas disciplinary conferences with a hearing officer are reserved exclusively for individual students.
Under what circumstances can an organization’s leaders be held accountable for misconduct?
Organization leaders, officers, or spokespersons may be held collectively and/or individually responsible if they gave tacit or overt consent or encouragement for prohibited conduct.
What happens if a student organization’s leaders ignore directives from the Dean of Students to address misconduct?

Failure by leaders, officers, or spokespersons to make reasonable efforts to comply with directives from the Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students or designee is considered a violation of the Code by both the individuals and the organization.
What kinds of sanctions can a student organization face for violating the Code of Conduct?
Sanctions may include revocation or denial of registration, as well as other appropriate sanctions listed in Sections 10, 11, and 12 of the Student Code of Conduct.
If my student organization hosts an event off-campus that’s sponsored by the university, can we still be held responsible for code violations that happen there?

Yes, according to Section 20.2 of the Student Handbook, student groups and registered student organizations may be held collectively and/or individually responsible for violations that occur at University-sponsored activities, whether on or off campus.
What if only a few members were involved in the misconduct—can the whole organization still be punished?

Yes. Under Section 20.3, your organization and its officers may be held collectively and/or individually responsible if leaders, officers, or spokespersons gave tacit or overt consent or encouragement for the prohibited conduct—even if not all members participated.
Our club received a directive from the Dean of Students to stop a certain activity because it might violate the Code. What happens if we don’t comply?

According to Section 20.4, failure to make reasonable efforts to comply with directives from the Assistant Vice President/Dean of Students or their designee is itself considered a violation of the Code—by both the organization and its leaders, officers, or spokespersons.
If we are found responsible, what kinds of sanctions could our organization face?

Per Section 20.5, possible sanctions include revocation or denial of your organization’s registration, as well as other appropriate sanctions listed in Sections 10, 11, and 12 of the Code—such as educational requirements, fines, suspension of privileges, or organizational probation.

I'm the president of a registered student organization, and during our recent off-campus event, some members got into a physical altercation. No one was seriously hurt, but campus security was called. Could our entire organization be held responsible for this?

Yes, your organization could be held collectively responsible. According to Section 20.2 of the Student Handbook, student organizations may be held accountable for violations that occur at University-sponsored activities—even off campus—or on premises controlled by the group. Additionally, under Section 20.3, if leaders (including you as president) gave tacit or overt approval of risky behavior, that could further implicate the organization. The matter would be reviewed by the University Hearing Board, not through an individual disciplinary conference.

I’m a freshman and just joined a student group last month. Last week, the group hosted a party where underage drinking occurred, and now the Dean’s office says they’re investigating the organization. I didn’t drink or encourage it—can I still get in trouble personally?

While the organization as a whole can be held responsible for the incident under Sections 20.2 and 20.3, individual responsibility depends on your specific actions. If you didn’t participate in or encourage the prohibited conduct, you’re less likely to face personal sanctions. However, the handbook notes that individuals may still be held accountable if they were present and failed to act when directed by university officials. Since you’re new and weren’t involved, cooperating fully with the investigation is your best course of action.
